title: Which map is the real map?
summary: In a Mexico City taxi, a driver and a tourist disagree about whether a phone map can show what a city actually is.

body.speaker: Tourist
body.text: Please, to this address. I have the map on my phone. I can show you.

body.speaker: Carlos
body.text: Put the phone down. I know the address.

body.speaker: Tourist
body.text: But the phone says this road is faster.

body.speaker: Carlos
body.text: Faster on the phone. Not faster in life. At this hour, that road has a market. The market closes at one. Now it is twelve.

body.speaker: Tourist
body.text: Oh. The phone does not know about the market.

body.speaker: Carlos
body.text: The phone knows the roads. It does not know the city.

body.speaker: Tourist
body.text: But the phone is updated every minute.

body.speaker: Carlos
body.text: Updated, yes. But by whom? By cars. The phone sees the cars. It does not see the children walking to school at eight. It does not see the rain in July at four. I see those things.

body.speaker: Tourist
body.text: You sound like you do not like phones.

body.speaker: Carlos
body.text: I like phones. I have one. My son built me an app. The app shows me where my grandchildren are. That is good. But the phone is a tool. A tool is not a city.

body.speaker: Tourist
body.text: I never thought about it like that.

body.speaker: Carlos
body.text: Most tourists do not. They arrive with a map. They leave with the same map. They never see what is between two streets.

body.speaker: Carlos
body.text: A bakery. A small park. A woman who sells flowers on Wednesdays. Your phone cannot tell you about her.

body.speaker: Tourist
body.text: Maybe I will walk back from the hotel later.

body.speaker: Carlos
body.text: Good. Leave the phone in the room. Take your eyes instead.

annotations.meaningVi: nhanh hơn (so sánh hơn của fast)
mang theo đôi mắt (cách nói: hãy quan sát bằng mắt mình)

grammarNotes.title: Negative present simple: 'does not + verb'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: A1 dùng 'does not + V-bare' với he/she/it. Trong bài: 'The phone does not know about the market.' 'It does not see the children walking to school.' 'Your phone cannot tell you about her.' Lưu ý: sau 'does not', động từ trở về dạng nguyên, không thêm -s. Câu rút gọn: doesn't.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: At A1 we form the negative with 'does not + bare verb' for he/she/it. From the passage: 'The phone does not know about the market.' 'It does not see the children walking to school.' After 'does not', the verb returns to its base form (no -s). Short form: doesn't.

translationVi:
Du khách: Cho tôi đến địa chỉ này. Tôi có bản đồ trong điện thoại. Tôi cho ông xem.
Carlos: Bỏ điện thoại xuống. Tôi biết địa chỉ này.
Du khách: Nhưng điện thoại bảo đường này nhanh hơn.
Carlos: Nhanh trên điện thoại. Không nhanh trong đời. Giờ này con đường đó có cái chợ. Chợ đóng lúc một giờ. Bây giờ mới mười hai.
Du khách: À. Điện thoại không biết về cái chợ.
Carlos: Điện thoại biết những con đường. Nó không biết thành phố.
Du khách: Nhưng điện thoại được cập nhật mỗi phút mà.
Carlos: Cập nhật, đúng. Nhưng bởi ai? Bởi xe hơi. Điện thoại thấy xe. Nó không thấy lũ trẻ đi học lúc tám giờ. Nó không thấy cơn mưa tháng Bảy lúc bốn giờ. Tôi thấy những thứ đó.
Du khách: Nghe như ông không thích điện thoại.
Carlos: Tôi thích điện thoại. Tôi có một cái. Con trai tôi làm cho tôi một ứng dụng. Ứng dụng đó cho tôi biết các cháu tôi đang ở đâu. Tốt đấy. Nhưng điện thoại là công cụ. Công cụ không phải là thành phố.
Du khách: Tôi chưa bao giờ nghĩ kiểu đó.
Carlos: Phần lớn du khách không nghĩ vậy. Họ đến với một bản đồ. Họ về với cùng bản đồ ấy. Họ không thấy cái gì nằm giữa hai con phố.
Du khách: Giữa hai con phố có gì?
Carlos: Một tiệm bánh. Một công viên nhỏ. Một người phụ nữ bán hoa vào thứ Tư. Điện thoại của anh không kể cho anh về bà ấy.
Du khách: Có lẽ chiều nay tôi sẽ đi bộ về từ khách sạn.
Carlos: Tốt. Để điện thoại trong phòng. Mang đôi mắt theo thôi.

questions.explanation: Implied throughout — Carlos is a taxi driver in Mexico City.
questions.hint: Look at the title and setting cues.

questions.prompt: Why does Carlos disagree with the phone's faster route?
options: There is a market on that road until one o'clock
He does not know that road
There is a tram on that road
questions.explanation: 'That road has a market. The market closes at one.'

questions.prompt: What time is it in the conversation?
According to Carlos, by whom is the phone map updated?
What does the phone NOT see, according to Carlos?
options: Children walking to school at eight; the rain in July at four
questions.explanation: 'It does not see the children walking to school at eight. It does not see the rain in July at four.'

options: No — he has one, and his son built him an app to track his grandchildren
Yes — he refuses to use any phone
He has never seen a phone
He sells phones on the side
questions.explanation: 'I like phones. I have one. My son built me an app.'

questions.prompt: What does Carlos mean by 'A tool is not a city'?
options: A useful device does not capture everything a place actually is

questions.prompt: What lives 'between two streets' according to Carlos?
options: A bakery, a small park, and a woman who sells flowers on Wednesdays

questions.prompt: What does Carlos suggest the tourist do?
options: Walk back from the hotel, leaving the phone in the room
questions.explanation: 'Leave the phone in the room. Take your eyes instead.'

options: A map shows roads; a place is also the people and rhythms that maps cannot record
Mexico City has no good roads
Taxi drivers know more than everyone
questions.explanation: Carlos's whole argument circles this — phones are tools, not cities.
questions.hint: Look at Carlos's 'A tool is not a city.'

Carlos: Updated, yes. But by whom? By {{b5}}. The phone sees the cars. It does not see the children walking to school at eight. It does not see the rain in July at four. I see those things.
Tourist: You sound like you do not like phones.
Carlos: I like phones. I have one. My son built me an app. The app shows me where my {{b6}} are. That is good. But the phone is a tool. A tool is not a {{b7}}.
Tourist: I never thought about it like that.
Carlos: Most tourists do not. They arrive with a map. They leave with the same map. They never see what is {{b8}} two streets.

blanks.explanation: The phone says this road is faster.
A bakery, a park, a flower seller.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Carlos says, 'They never see what is between two streets.' Think about your own neighbourhood. Name one thing between two streets near your home that a map would not show — and would you be sad if it disappeared?
